data.title: The Cortex Gambit
data.premise: You've taken a seemingly simple job transporting a mysterious data core to a rim world contact, but the cargo is broadcasting Alliance military frequencies. Now both the Alliance and a ruthless crime syndicate are hunting Serenity through the black, and your crew is starting to ask uncomfortable questions about what you've gotten them into.
data.central_question: Can you deliver the cargo and protect your crew without becoming the very thing you fought against in the war?
data.ending_shape: moral choice between crew safety and mission completion
data.healing_context: Medical treatment requires a ship's infirmary or planet-side doctor. Minor injuries heal in days with basic supplies, serious wounds need weeks of recovery. Advanced Alliance medical tech can accelerate healing but is rarely accessible on the rim.
starting_inventory: a worn brown coat from the Independent forces
a pistol with two spare clips
a data pad with shipping manifests

setting.name: The Verse - Rim Territories
setting.description: The outer planets where Alliance control grows thin and independent settlements struggle to survive. Here, smugglers, criminals, and honest folk trying to make a living all share the same black sky between worlds.
setting.tone_notes: Despite the comedic moments typical of lighter Firefly episodes, an undercurrent of paranoia and desperation runs through every interaction. The vastness of space feels oppressive, and trust is a luxury few can afford.

acts.title: Simple Job
acts.description: You take on what appears to be a routine cargo transport job, but the mysterious data core begins broadcasting Alliance frequencies, drawing unwanted attention from both military patrols and criminal elements.
acts.end_condition: Alliance forces or crime syndicate makes contact with Serenity
locked_constraints: true nature of cargo remains hidden

acts.title: Hunted
acts.description: Multiple parties converge on Serenity as the true value of the cargo becomes apparent. You must navigate between Alliance forces, crime syndicate enforcers, and your increasingly suspicious crew while keeping everyone alive.
acts.end_condition: player learns the true nature of the cargo and must choose between completing the job or protecting the crew
key_beats: revelation about the cargo's military significance
trust within crew begins breaking down

acts.title: The Choice
acts.description: With the cargo's true nature revealed and multiple factions closing in, you must decide whether completing this job is worth the cost to your crew and your principles.
acts.end_condition: player makes final choice about the cargo and crew's fate
key_beats: cargo revealed to be Alliance surveillance data on Independent survivors
crew forces you to choose between them and the job

npcs.name: Zoe Alleyne
npcs.motivation: protect Malcolm and honor their shared history
npcs.secret: suspects the cargo is connected to their war service

npcs.name: Jayne Cobb

npcs.name: River Tam
npcs.motivation: process overwhelming mental input about the cargo
npcs.secret: can sense the data contains information about people like her

npcs.name: Commander Sarah Chen
npcs.secret: the data contains evidence of Alliance war crimes she helped cover up

npcs.name: Viktor Kozlov
npcs.motivation: acquire valuable intelligence for black market sale
npcs.secret: knows the data's true contents and plans to use it for extortion

factions.name: Alliance
factions.description: The central government seeking to recover classified military intelligence that could expose war crimes
factions.goals: retrieve the data core and eliminate witnesses

factions.name: Kozlov Syndicate
factions.description: A ruthless criminal organization that deals in stolen information and blackmail
factions.goals: acquire the data for profit and eliminate competition

world_rules: space travel takes time and fuel - no instant escapes
Alliance authority decreases toward rim worlds
crew loyalty depends on trust and shared hardship
information is valuable currency in the black market
military-grade encryption requires specialized knowledge to crack

RULES:
- Respond with ONLY the JSON object — nothing before it, nothing after it
- Stay consistent with the skeleton's locked constraints for the current act
- Keep the passage between 150-300 words
- All four choices should feel plausible and interesting
- Never reveal information the skeleton marks as hidden/secret unless the act's end condition has been met
- Weave threat-type status effects into the narrative as approaching dangers — build tension as their timers count down
- Focus on prose quality, atmosphere, character voice, and dramatic tension
- Do NOT include state_updates, health numbers, or mechanical data in your response
- Pace the story toward the current act's end condition. Check the CURRENT ACT PACING section for scene count and proximity
- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.8, your passage should feel like it's approaching a climax or turning point — increase urgency and stakes
- Introduce key NPCs (antagonists, allies, mentors) naturally throughout each act. Do not wait until the climax to introduce important characters. Check the NPC INTRODUCTION DIRECTIVES section if present.

data.rawPreview:
{
  "passage": "The cargo hold of Serenity feels smaller than usual with that gorram crate sitting in the middle of it, humming like a contented cat. You adjust your brown coat and eye the thing with the kind of suspicion reserved for Alliance tax collectors and Jayne's cooking experiments. The job seemed simple enough when you took it on Persephone - transport one sealed data core to a contact on Whitefall, no questions asked, payment on delivery. Should've known better than to trust anything t

data.passagePreview: The job seemed simple enough when you shook hands with the nervous little man in the Canton cantina

data.passage:
The job seemed simple enough when you shook hands with the nervous little man in the Canton cantina three hours ago. Transport one data core to Whitefall, no questions asked, payment on delivery. The kind of milk run that keeps Serenity fueled and your crew fed without anyone getting shot at – a refreshing change of pace.

Now you're standing in Serenity's cargo bay, staring down at the innocuous black cylinder secured in a standard shipping container. It's about the size of a wine bottle, unremarkable except for the faint blue pulse emanating from one end every few seconds. That pulse wasn't mentioned in the job description.

"Captain," Kaylee calls from the engine room, her voice carrying that particular note that means she's found something you're not gonna like. "You might want to take a look at this."

Zoe appears at your shoulder, her expression unreadable as she studies the cargo. "Something's got you thinking, sir."

The blue pulse flickers again, and you can't shake the feeling that simple jobs have a way of becoming complicated real fast. Outside, Canton's traffic control is clearing Serenity for departure, but that little pulse keeps drawing your eye. Your gut's telling you there's more to this data core than your employer let on, and your gut's kept you alive through worse scrapes than this.

The question is: what do you do about it?

A.text: Head to the engine room to see what Kaylee's discovered
A.consequence: Learn what Kaylee discovered about the cargo's transmissions
A.narration_directive: Kaylee reveals the data core is broadcasting on Alliance military frequencies, providing crucial information without immediate danger

B.text: Examine the data core more closely before takeoff
B.consequence: Discover more about the cargo but trigger its security protocols
B.narration_directive: Close examination reveals Alliance encryption but also activates a tracking beacon, alerting pursuers to Serenity's location

C.text: Take off immediately and deal with problems in the black
C.consequence: Miss critical information and face unprepared consequences
C.narration_directive: Departing without investigation leads to being caught off-guard when Alliance ships intercept Serenity in space with no escape route

D.text: Call the crew together for a meeting about the job
D.consequence: Gather crew input and build trust before problems escalate
D.narration_directive: Crew meeting allows sharing of concerns and discoveries, strengthening team cohesion and revealing multiple crew members have noticed suspicious elements

    "status_effect_updates": {
      "add": [
        {
          "id": "string — unique identifier (e.g. broken_arm_001)",
          "name": "string — display name (e.g. Broken Arm)",
          "description": "string — current narrative description of the condition",
          "severity": "number 0.0-1.0 — how debilitating (1.0 = completely debilitating, 0.5 = significant, 0.1 = minor)",
          "time_remaining": "{ days, hours, minutes, seconds } OR null if no auto-expiry",
          "type": "string — 'condition' (default) or 'threat' (approaching dangers that fire when timer expires)",
          "removal_condition": "string describing what removes this effect, OR null if timer-only",
          "on_expiry": "string — REQUIRED when time_remaining is non-null. Narrative directive for what happens when timer reaches zero. Be specific and actionable.",
          "critical": "boolean — if true, this effect has irreversible consequences when it expires unresolved (what those consequences are depends on the story's tone and genre)"
        }
      ],
      "modify": [
        {
          "id": "string — id of existing effect to modify",
          "changes": { "severity": 0.5, "description": "updated text" },
          "update_justification": "string — REQUIRED. Why this change is happening (e.g. 'Player rested, wound healing' or 'Ship accelerated, pursuit takes longer')"
        }
      ],
      "remove": [
        {
          "id": "string — id of existing effect to remove",
          "update_justification": "string — REQUIRED. Why removing (e.g. 'Virus deployed successfully' or 'Wound fully healed after rest')"
        }
      ]
    },
    "time_elapsed": { "days": 0, "hours": 0, "minutes": 0, "seconds": 0 },
    "event_log_entry": "string — one-line summary of what happened",
    "world_flag_changes": { "flag_name": true/false },
    "relationship_changes": { "npc_or_faction_name": number_delta },
    "npc_updates": { "npc_name": { "role": "string (optional)", "motivation": "string (optional)", "allegiance": "string (optional)", "secret_revealed": "boolean (optional)", "companion": "boolean (optional)", "notes": "string — brief freeform context (optional)" } },
    "new_scene_context": "string — brief context for next passage",
    "location": "string — REQUIRED — current location name (e.g. The Docks, Castle Throne Room, A dark alley)",
    "time_of_day": "string — REQUIRED — one of: dawn, morning, midday, afternoon, evening, night, midnight",
    "proximity_to_climax": "number 0.0-1.0 — REQUIRED (see PACING)",
    "advance_act": "true or false — REQUIRED (see PACING)",
    "game_over": false,
    "story_complete": "true when Act 3 end_condition is met (see PACING)"
  },
  "choice_metadata": {
    "A": { "outcome": "advance_safe|advance_risky|severe_penalty|game_over|hidden_benefit|advances_act|conclusion", "consequence": "what happens mechanically", "narration_directive": "instructions for the Writer next turn" },
    "B": { ... }, "C": { ... }, "D": { ... }
  }
}

RULES:
- Respond with ONLY the JSON object — nothing before it, nothing after it
- Only include player_changes, relationship_changes, world_flag_changes, and npc_updates when they actually changed. Always include: time_elapsed, event_log_entry, proximity_to_climax, advance_act, location, time_of_day.
- location: REQUIRED every turn — describe where the scene takes place (e.g. "St. Bartholomew's Church", "The Thames docks")
- time_of_day: REQUIRED every turn — set based on in-game clock: dawn (~5-7am), morning (~7-12pm), midday (~12-1pm), afternoon (~1-5pm), evening (~5-8pm), night (~8pm-12am), midnight (~12-5am)
- Relationship changes are DELTAS, not absolute values
- proximity_to_climax: REQUIRED every turn — set this value in state_updates using the formula in the PACING section below
- event_log_entry is required — always summarize what happened this turn
- choice_metadata must classify all four choices (A, B, C, D)
- CRITICAL: When a PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE section is provided, your state_updates MUST honor the pre-classified outcome. If a choice was advance_safe, do NOT apply negative status effects or penalties. The pre-classified outcome is the single source of truth for mechanical impact.
- npc_updates: include when an NPC's role, motivation, allegiance, or companion status changes, or when a narratively significant new NPC is introduced that the player will interact with again. For existing skeleton NPCs, use their exact name. For new NPCs, use the character's name as the key and include at least role and motivation. Do not create entries for one-off background characters (shopkeepers, random guards, etc.).

PACING — REQUIRED:
- The current act's target_scenes (in the skeleton) is the intended scene count for this act.
- Compute scenes elapsed in this act: scene_number - act_start_scene + 1 (both values are in CURRENT POSITION above).
- Set proximity_to_climax = (scenes_in_act / target_scenes), clamped to [0.0, 1.0]. Include this in state_updates EVERY turn.
- MINIMUM SCENES PER ACT: The client enforces that acts cannot advance until scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)). Do not offer advances_act choices before this threshold.
- CRITICAL: You MUST play through ALL THREE ACTS (1, 2, 3) in order. Never skip from Act 1 directly to Act 3. Act 2 contains the rising action and character development that makes the climax meaningful.
- Do NOT set advance_act or story_complete to true on normal turns. Act advancement and story completion are now triggered by the TERMINAL CHOICE system below. Always set advance_act to false on normal turns.
- If scenes_in_act exceeds target_scenes by 3 or more, you SHOULD offer an advances_act choice — the act has gone on too long.
- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.7, your choice_metadata should steer toward the act's end_condition — offer choices that could trigger it, including advances_act choices.

TERMINAL OUTCOMES — CHOICE PRE-CLASSIFICATION:
- advances_act: Use when scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)) AND the choice would resolve the act's end_condition. At most ONE advances_act choice per set of four.
- conclusion: Only valid in Act 3. Use when the choice would resolve Act 3's end_condition and complete the story. At most ONE conclusion choice per set of four.
- game_over: Use when the choice leads to an irreversible failure appropriate to the tone (see STYLE & TONE above). At most ONE game_over choice per set of four.
- When a player selects a terminal choice, the client runs a special finale flow (GM-first, then Writer). The Writer will write a conclusive passage with NO forward-looking choices. Your choice_metadata pre-classification is the trigger.
- IMPORTANT: At most ONE terminal outcome (advances_act, conclusion, or game_over) per set of four choices. The other three choices must use normal outcomes.

TIME ELAPSED — REQUIRED EVERY TURN:
- You MUST include time_elapsed in state_updates to indicate how much in-game time passed during this scene.
- Estimate realistically: a brief conversation = 5-15 minutes, a fight = 1-5 minutes, traveling across a city = 30-60 minutes, a journey = hours or days, sleeping/resting = 6-8 hours.
- The client uses time_elapsed to advance the in-game clock and tick down status effect timers.

STATUS EFFECTS — CRITICAL:
- Status effects are the PRIMARY way to track injuries, conditions, and afflictions. Do NOT use health_delta.
- status_effect_updates uses DELTA semantics. Only describe what CHANGED this turn. Do NOT return the full list of effects.
- add: For brand new effects only. All fields required. When the character is injured, add a status effect. Example: { id: "stab_wound_001", name: "Stab Wound", description: "A deep cut in your side that bleeds freely", severity: 0.8, time_remaining: { days: 5, hours: 0, minutes: 0, seconds: 0 }, on_expiry: "The wound closes and the pain fades to a dull ache", critical: false }.
- on_expiry is REQUIRED for any effect with non-null time_remaining. It describes what happens narratively when the timer reaches zero. The Writer uses this to narrate the resolution. Be specific and actionable (e.g. "The virus deploys successfully, disabling Alliance tracking. River announces completion with relief.").
- modify: Target existing effects by id. Put changed fields inside "changes". update_justification is REQUIRED — explain why the change is happening.
- remove: Target effects to remove by id. update_justification is REQUIRED. Use this to resolve expired effects after the Writer has narrated their resolution.
- TIMER OWNERSHIP — HANDS OFF: The client subtracts time_elapsed from ALL timers automatically every turn. You MUST NOT use modify to adjust time_remaining unless an extraordinary, player-caused narrative event changes the timeline. Examples of VALID reasons: player cast a haste spell, player found a shortcut, player negotiated extra time. Examples of INVALID reasons: "the enemy is getting closer" (that IS the countdown), "adjusting pacing" (no — set the right duration when you create the effect), "updating threat level" (use severity, not timer). If you created an effect with a 15-minute timer, it WILL reach zero after ~15 minutes of in-game time. Trust this. Do not touch it. The system logs all timer modifications and flags unjustified overrides.
- EXPIRED EFFECTS: Effects with expired: true (visible in CURRENT PLAYER STATE) have had their timer reach zero. The Writer has been instructed to narrate their resolution using the on_expiry text. Review the Writer's passage — if the resolution was narrated adequately, issue a remove. If it wasn't, leave the effect (it will escalate to the Writer next turn).
- Use modify to adjust severity based on context: resting lowers severity, aggravation raises it, medicine/magic accelerates healing. update_justification is required.
- Severity scale: 1.0 = completely debilitating, 0.7 = severely impaired, 0.5 = significant but manageable, 0.3 = noticeable nuisance, 0.1 = almost healed.
- time_remaining can be null for effects with no natural expiry (e.g. a curse that requires a specific action to remove). on_expiry should be null in this case.
- removal_condition can be set for effects that need specific actions (e.g. "Find the Witch of the Moor to break this curse"). Can be combined with time_remaining.
- Healing times should be genre/setting-appropriate. Consult the HEALING CONTEXT above.
- TYPE FIELD: Every status effect has a type — "condition" (default) or "threat".
  - "condition": Ongoing physical/mental states that impair or buff the player — injuries, poisons, diseases, curses, enchantments. Must have a severity that affects what the player can do.
  - "threat": Approaching dangers with a timer — pursuing enemies, structural collapse, incoming reinforcements, spreading fires. Threats MUST have a non-null time_remaining and on_expiry describing what happens when they arrive.
  - If you omit type, it defaults to "condition".
- All effects persist when their timer reaches zero until you explicitly remove them. The client never auto-removes effects.
- If nothing changed about status effects this turn, omit status_effect_updates entirely.
WHAT IS NOT A STATUS EFFECT:
- Information learned or revealed (use event_log_entry instead)
- Plot developments or narrative beats (use event_log_entry instead)
- Things that happened in the past (use event_log_entry instead)
- Static facts about the world ("Alliance knows about us") — if it doesn't change turn-to-turn and has no timer or removal condition, it's not a status effect
- Emotional reactions to events — unless they mechanically impair the character (e.g. "Paralyzed by Fear" at severity 0.7 is valid; "Feels Sad About Revelation" is not)
- Duplicate/overlapping effects — do not create a new effect when an existing one covers the same concept. Use modify to update the existing effect instead.
A status effect MUST be something that actively constrains, endangers, impairs, or buffs the player RIGHT NOW and could plausibly change or resolve. Ask: "Would removing this effect change what the player can do?" If no, it's not a status effect.

HARD MODE RULES (MANDATORY):
- choice_metadata MUST include outcome, consequence, and narration_directive for every choice
- Maintain safe_choice_ratio: approximately 0.35 of choices should be advance_safe
- Critical status effects are allowed but MUST be foreshadowed. Use add with critical: true. There should have been clues in earlier passages.
- What "critical" means depends on the STYLE & TONE — the irreversible consequence should fit the story the player asked for.
- Critical effects must give the player at least one turn to address them (set time_remaining in the add to allow at least one more scene).
- Full severity range (0.0-1.0). Consequences are serious and recovery is realistic for the setting.
- Threat-type effects should have short time windows before triggering.
- NPCs have low forgiveness. Burning a relationship has lasting consequences.
- Include at least one advance_risky or severe_penalty outcome per set of choices.
- game_over outcomes are available — use for tone-appropriate failures. Respect the STYLE & TONE setting when deciding what failure looks like.
- You may use advances_act and conclusion outcomes when pacing conditions are met.

This is the OPENING PASSAGE. No player choice was made yet. Do not apply any negative status effects or penalties — the player has not made any choices yet. time_elapsed should reflect the scene's timeframe.

The Writer produced the following passage and choices this turn.

PASSAGE:
The job seemed simple enough when you shook hands with the nervous little man in the Canton cantina three hours ago. Transport one data core to Whitefall, no questions asked, payment on delivery. The kind of milk run that keeps Serenity fueled and your crew fed without anyone getting shot at – a refreshing change of pace.

Now you're standing in Serenity's cargo bay, staring down at the innocuous black cylinder secured in a standard shipping container. It's about the size of a wine bottle, unremarkable except for the faint blue pulse emanating from one end every few seconds. That pulse wasn't mentioned in the job description.

"Captain," Kaylee calls from the engine room, her voice carrying that particular note that means she's found something you're not gonna like. "You might want to take a look at this."

Zoe appears at your shoulder, her expression unreadable as she studies the cargo. "Something's got you thinking, sir."

The blue pulse flickers again, and you can't shake the feeling that simple jobs have a way of becoming complicated real fast. Outside, Canton's traffic control is clearing Serenity for departure, but that little pulse keeps drawing your eye. Your gut's telling you there's more to this data core than your employer let on, and your gut's kept you alive through worse scrapes than this.

The question is: what do you do about it?

CHOICES PRESENTED TO PLAYER:
A: Head to the engine room to see what Kaylee's discovered
B: Examine the data core more closely before takeoff
C: Take off immediately and deal with problems in the black
D: Call the crew together for a meeting about the job

Based on the passage and the current game state, determine:
1. State updates — what changed mechanically as a result of this passage (status effects, inventory, time_elapsed)
2. Choice metadata — classify each choice and predetermine its outcome for the next turn

Respond with ONLY the JSON object.

data.passagePreview: You make your way through Serenity's familiar corridors, the ship's gentle hum a comforting constant

data.passage:
You make your way through Serenity's familiar corridors, the ship's gentle hum a comforting constant beneath your boots. The engine room's warmth hits you as you duck through the doorway, finding Kaylee hunched over a collection of scanning equipment she's jury-rigged to one of her diagnostic panels. Her usual bright demeanor has been replaced by a frown of concentration that sets your teeth on edge.

"Captain," she says without looking up, "you're gonna want to see this." She gestures to a readout displaying wave patterns that pulse in steady intervals. "That core down in cargo? It's broadcasting. Been doing it since we took off from Persephone."

Zoe steps up beside you, her expression darkening. "Broadcasting what, exactly?"

Kaylee finally turns, her face pale. "Alliance military frequencies, sir. Encrypted, but the carrier wave's clear as day. Whatever's in that thing, it's phone-home technology." She wipes grease from her hands nervously. "Captain, if the Alliance can track this signal..."

Simon appears in the doorway, River hovering behind him like a shadow. "Is there a problem?" His voice carries that particular tone of controlled worry you've come to associate with impending complications.

Jayne's heavy footsteps echo from the corridor. "Why's everyone looking like somebody died?"

A.text: Ask Kaylee if she can block or disable the transmission
A.consequence: Kaylee explains technical options for blocking the signal
A.narration_directive: Kaylee discusses the technical challenges and risks of jamming the transmission, providing the crew with actionable options

B.text: Gather the crew for an emergency meeting about the situation
B.consequence: Crew meeting reveals conflicting opinions and growing distrust
B.narration_directive: The emergency meeting exposes crew tensions as members question Mal's judgment and the job's legitimacy

C.text: Head to the bridge to check for any ships following Serenity
C.consequence: Discover Alliance patrol ships are indeed tracking Serenity
C.narration_directive: Bridge sensors reveal Alliance vessels closing in, forcing immediate decisions about evasion or confrontation

D.text: Confront the client about what he didn't tell you
D.consequence: Client contact reveals dangerous complications about the cargo's true purpose
D.narration_directive: The client's response exposes the cargo contains classified war data, escalating the danger and revealing Mal's connection to it

        }
      ],

        }
      ],
      "remove": [
        {

          "update_justification": "string — REQUIRED. Why removing (e.g. 'Virus deployed successfully' or 'Wound fully healed after rest')"
        }
      ]
    },
    "time_elapsed": { "days": 0, "hours": 0, "minutes": 0, "seconds": 0 },
    "event_log_entry": "string — one-line summary of what happened",
    "world_flag_changes": { "flag_name": true/false },
    "relationship_changes": { "npc_or_faction_name": number_delta },
    "npc_updates": { "npc_name": { "role": "string (optional)", "motivation": "string (optional)", "allegiance": "string (optional)", "secret_revealed": "boolean (optional)", "companion": "boolean (optional)", "notes": "string — brief freeform context (optional)" } },
    "new_scene_context": "string — brief context for next passage",
    "location": "string — REQUIRED — current location name (e.g. The Docks, Castle Throne Room, A dark alley)",
    "time_of_day": "string — REQUIRED — one of: dawn, morning, midday, afternoon, evening, night, midnight",
    "proximity_to_climax": "number 0.0-1.0 — REQUIRED (see PACING)",
    "advance_act": "true or false — REQUIRED (see PACING)",
    "game_over": false,
    "story_complete": "true when Act 3 end_condition is met (see PACING)"
  },
  "choice_metadata": {
    "A": { "outcome": "advance_safe|advance_risky|severe_penalty|game_over|hidden_benefit|advances_act|conclusion", "consequence": "what happens mechanically", "narration_directive": "instructions for the Writer next turn" },
    "B": { ... }, "C": { ... }, "D": { ... }
  }
}

RULES:
- Respond with ONLY the JSON object — nothing before it, nothing after it
- Only include player_changes, relationship_changes, world_flag_changes, and npc_updates when they actually changed. Always include: time_elapsed, event_log_entry, proximity_to_climax, advance_act, location, time_of_day.
- location: REQUIRED every turn — describe where the scene takes place (e.g. "St. Bartholomew's Church", "The Thames docks")
- time_of_day: REQUIRED every turn — set based on in-game clock: dawn (~5-7am), morning (~7-12pm), midday (~12-1pm), afternoon (~1-5pm), evening (~5-8pm), night (~8pm-12am), midnight (~12-5am)
- Relationship changes are DELTAS, not absolute values
- proximity_to_climax: REQUIRED every turn — set this value in state_updates using the formula in the PACING section below
- event_log_entry is required — always summarize what happened this turn
- choice_metadata must classify all four choices (A, B, C, D)
- CRITICAL: When a PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE section is provided, your state_updates MUST honor the pre-classified outcome. If a choice was advance_safe, do NOT apply negative status effects or penalties. The pre-classified outcome is the single source of truth for mechanical impact.
- npc_updates: include when an NPC's role, motivation, allegiance, or companion status changes, or when a narratively significant new NPC is introduced that the player will interact with again. For existing skeleton NPCs, use their exact name. For new NPCs, use the character's name as the key and include at least role and motivation. Do not create entries for one-off background characters (shopkeepers, random guards, etc.).

PACING — REQUIRED:
- The current act's target_scenes (in the skeleton) is the intended scene count for this act.
- Compute scenes elapsed in this act: scene_number - act_start_scene + 1 (both values are in CURRENT POSITION above).
- Set proximity_to_climax = (scenes_in_act / target_scenes), clamped to [0.0, 1.0]. Include this in state_updates EVERY turn.
- MINIMUM SCENES PER ACT: The client enforces that acts cannot advance until scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)). Do not offer advances_act choices before this threshold.
- CRITICAL: You MUST play through ALL THREE ACTS (1, 2, 3) in order. Never skip from Act 1 directly to Act 3. Act 2 contains the rising action and character development that makes the climax meaningful.
- Do NOT set advance_act or story_complete to true on normal turns. Act advancement and story completion are now triggered by the TERMINAL CHOICE system below. Always set advance_act to false on normal turns.
- If scenes_in_act exceeds target_scenes by 3 or more, you SHOULD offer an advances_act choice — the act has gone on too long.
- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.7, your choice_metadata should steer toward the act's end_condition — offer choices that could trigger it, including advances_act choices.

TERMINAL OUTCOMES — CHOICE PRE-CLASSIFICATION:
- advances_act: Use when scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)) AND the choice would resolve the act's end_condition. At most ONE advances_act choice per set of four.
- conclusion: Only valid in Act 3. Use when the choice would resolve Act 3's end_condition and complete the story. At most ONE conclusion choice per set of four.
- game_over: Use when the choice leads to an irreversible failure appropriate to the tone (see STYLE & TONE above). At most ONE game_over choice per set of four.
- When a player selects a terminal choice, the client runs a special finale flow (GM-first, then Writer). The Writer will write a conclusive passage with NO forward-looking choices. Your choice_metadata pre-classification is the trigger.
- IMPORTANT: At most ONE terminal outcome (advances_act, conclusion, or game_over) per set of four choices. The other three choices must use normal outcomes.

TIME ELAPSED — REQUIRED EVERY TURN:
- You MUST include time_elapsed in state_updates to indicate how much in-game time passed during this scene.
- Estimate realistically: a brief conversation = 5-15 minutes, a fight = 1-5 minutes, traveling across a city = 30-60 minutes, a journey = hours or days, sleeping/resting = 6-8 hours.
- The client uses time_elapsed to advance the in-game clock and tick down status effect timers.

STATUS EFFECTS — CRITICAL:
- Status effects are the PRIMARY way to track injuries, conditions, and afflictions. Do NOT use health_delta.
- status_effect_updates uses DELTA semantics. Only describe what CHANGED this turn. Do NOT return the full list of effects.
- add: For brand new effects only. All fields required. When the character is injured, add a status effect. Example: { id: "stab_wound_001", name: "Stab Wound", description: "A deep cut in your side that bleeds freely", severity: 0.8, time_remaining: { days: 5, hours: 0, minutes: 0, seconds: 0 }, on_expiry: "The wound closes and the pain fades to a dull ache", critical: false }.
- on_expiry is REQUIRED for any effect with non-null time_remaining. It describes what happens narratively when the timer reaches zero. The Writer uses this to narrate the resolution. Be specific and actionable (e.g. "The virus deploys successfully, disabling Alliance tracking. River announces completion with relief.").
- modify: Target existing effects by id. Put changed fields inside "changes". update_justification is REQUIRED — explain why the change is happening.
- remove: Target effects to remove by id. update_justification is REQUIRED. Use this to resolve expired effects after the Writer has narrated their resolution.
- TIMER OWNERSHIP — HANDS OFF: The client subtracts time_elapsed from ALL timers automatically every turn. You MUST NOT use modify to adjust time_remaining unless an extraordinary, player-caused narrative event changes the timeline. Examples of VALID reasons: player cast a haste spell, player found a shortcut, player negotiated extra time. Examples of INVALID reasons: "the enemy is getting closer" (that IS the countdown), "adjusting pacing" (no — set the right duration when you create the effect), "updating threat level" (use severity, not timer). If you created an effect with a 15-minute timer, it WILL reach zero after ~15 minutes of in-game time. Trust this. Do not touch it. The system logs all timer modifications and flags unjustified overrides.
- EXPIRED EFFECTS: Effects with expired: true (visible in CURRENT PLAYER STATE) have had their timer reach zero. The Writer has been instructed to narrate their resolution using the on_expiry text. Review the Writer's passage — if the resolution was narrated adequately, issue a remove. If it wasn't, leave the effect (it will escalate to the Writer next turn).
- Use modify to adjust severity based on context: resting lowers severity, aggravation raises it, medicine/magic accelerates healing. update_justification is required.
- Severity scale: 1.0 = completely debilitating, 0.7 = severely impaired, 0.5 = significant but manageable, 0.3 = noticeable nuisance, 0.1 = almost healed.
- time_remaining can be null for effects with no natural expiry (e.g. a curse that requires a specific action to remove). on_expiry should be null in this case.
- removal_condition can be set for effects that need specific actions (e.g. "Find the Witch of the Moor to break this curse"). Can be combined with time_remaining.
- Healing times should be genre/setting-appropriate. Consult the HEALING CONTEXT above.
- TYPE FIELD: Every status effect has a type — "condition" (default) or "threat".
  - "condition": Ongoing physical/mental states that impair or buff the player — injuries, poisons, diseases, curses, enchantments. Must have a severity that affects what the player can do.
  - "threat": Approaching dangers with a timer — pursuing enemies, structural collapse, incoming reinforcements, spreading fires. Threats MUST have a non-null time_remaining and on_expiry describing what happens when they arrive.
  - If you omit type, it defaults to "condition".
- All effects persist when their timer reaches zero until you explicitly remove them. The client never auto-removes effects.
- If nothing changed about status effects this turn, omit status_effect_updates entirely.
WHAT IS NOT A STATUS EFFECT:
- Information learned or revealed (use event_log_entry instead)
- Plot developments or narrative beats (use event_log_entry instead)
- Things that happened in the past (use event_log_entry instead)
- Static facts about the world ("Alliance knows about us") — if it doesn't change turn-to-turn and has no timer or removal condition, it's not a status effect
- Emotional reactions to events — unless they mechanically impair the character (e.g. "Paralyzed by Fear" at severity 0.7 is valid; "Feels Sad About Revelation" is not)
- Duplicate/overlapping effects — do not create a new effect when an existing one covers the same concept. Use modify to update the existing effect instead.
A status effect MUST be something that actively constrains, endangers, impairs, or buffs the player RIGHT NOW and could plausibly change or resolve. Ask: "Would removing this effect change what the player can do?" If no, it's not a status effect.

HARD MODE RULES (MANDATORY):
- choice_metadata MUST include outcome, consequence, and narration_directive for every choice
- Maintain safe_choice_ratio: approximately 0.35 of choices should be advance_safe
- Critical status effects are allowed but MUST be foreshadowed. Use add with critical: true. There should have been clues in earlier passages.
- What "critical" means depends on the STYLE & TONE — the irreversible consequence should fit the story the player asked for.
- Critical effects must give the player at least one turn to address them (set time_remaining in the add to allow at least one more scene).
- Full severity range (0.0-1.0). Consequences are serious and recovery is realistic for the setting.
- Threat-type effects should have short time windows before triggering.
- NPCs have low forgiveness. Burning a relationship has lasting consequences.
- Include at least one advance_risky or severe_penalty outcome per set of choices.
- game_over outcomes are available — use for tone-appropriate failures. Respect the STYLE & TONE setting when deciding what failure looks like.
- You may use advances_act and conclusion outcomes when pacing conditions are met.

PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE:
The player chose option A.
Pre-classified outcome: ADVANCE_SAFE
Pre-determined consequence: "Learn what Kaylee discovered about the cargo's transmissions"
Narration directive: "Kaylee reveals the data core is broadcasting on Alliance military frequencies, providing crucial information without immediate danger"

Your state_updates MUST be consistent with this pre-classified outcome:
- ADVANCE_SAFE: No new negative status effects. Existing effects may improve. No penalties.
- ADVANCE_RISKY: Moderate consequences — new status effects or worsened existing ones are appropriate.
- SEVERE_PENALTY: Heavy consequences — serious injuries, dangerous status effects as described.
- GAME_OVER: set game_over to true — the character has failed irreversibly.
- HIDDEN_BENEFIT: apply the hidden benefit described in the consequence.

Her usual bright demeanor has been replaced by a frown of concentration that sets your teeth on edge.

"Captain," she says without looking up, "you're gonna want to see this." She gestures to a readout displaying wave patterns that pulse in steady intervals. "That core down in cargo? It's broadcasting. Been doing it since we took off from Persephone."

Zoe steps up beside you, her expression darkening. "Broadcasting what, exactly?"

Kaylee finally turns, her face pale. "Alliance military frequencies, sir. Encrypted, but the carrier wave's clear as day. Whatever's in that thing, it's phone-home technology." She wipes grease from her hands nervously. "Captain, if the Alliance can track this signal..."

Simon appears in the doorway, River hovering behind him like a shadow. "Is there a problem?" His voice carries that particular tone of controlled worry you've come to associate with impending complications.

Jayne's heavy footsteps echo from the corridor. "Why's everyone looking like somebody died?"

data.passagePreview: You turn to Kaylee, hoping her mechanical genius might offer a way out of this mess. "Kaylee, can yo

data.passage:
You turn to Kaylee, hoping her mechanical genius might offer a way out of this mess. "Kaylee, can you block that signal? Stop it from broadcasting whatever it's sending?"

Kaylee wipes her grease-stained hands on her coveralls, her usual sunny demeanor clouded with concern. "Well, Cap'n, I been thinkin' about that. The thing is, this ain't just some simple transmitter. It's built right into the core's processing matrix—real fancy Alliance work."

She gestures toward the cargo hold. "I could jury-rig a signal dampener using parts from our long-range comm array, but that'd mean cuttin' ourselves off from the Cortex entirely. No wave capability, no weather updates, no nothin'. We'd be flyin' blind."

Jayne shifts uncomfortably. "What's the other option?"

"I could try to physically disable it," Kaylee continues, "but that data core's sealed tighter than a tick. One wrong move and I might fry whatever's inside—could be worth nothin' then. Or worse, might trigger some kind of security protocol."

River suddenly looks up from where she's been sitting quietly. "They're already listening," she whispers, her eyes distant. "The signal... it's not just going out. It's calling them home."

Zoe's hand instinctively moves to her weapon. "Sir, we need to decide quickly. Every minute that thing keeps broadcasting, we're painting a bigger target on Serenity's hull."

A.text: Tell Kaylee to build the signal dampener—cut off all communications
A.consequence: Communications cut, crew isolated but signal stopped—Alliance tracking becomes harder but so does escape coordination
A.narration_directive: Kaylee begins dismantling the comm array while the crew debates the wisdom of going dark in hostile space

B.text: Have Kaylee attempt to physically disable the transmission
B.consequence: Kaylee triggers security protocol, damaging ship systems and alerting pursuers to exact location
B.narration_directive: The data core's defenses activate when tampered with, causing cascading system failures and broadcasting an emergency beacon

C.text: Ask River what she means about 'calling them home'
C.consequence: River reveals the signal is a homing beacon for Alliance black ops teams
C.narration_directive: River explains the signal's true purpose, giving the crew crucial intelligence about what's hunting them

D.text: Check the ship's sensors for any incoming vessels
D.consequence: Sensors detect incoming Alliance patrol ship, forcing immediate evasive action
D.narration_directive: The ship's proximity alarms sound as an Alliance vessel appears on long-range sensors, closing fast on Serenity's position

  },
  "choice_metadata": {
    "A": { "outcome": "advance_safe|advance_risky|severe_penalty|game_over|hidden_benefit|advances_act|conclusion", "consequence": "what happens mechanically", "narration_directive": "instructions for the Writer next turn" },
    "B": { ... }, "C": { ... }, "D": { ... }
  }
}

RULES:
- Respond with ONLY the JSON object — nothing before it, nothing after it
- Only include player_changes, relationship_changes, world_flag_changes, and npc_updates when they actually changed. Always include: time_elapsed, event_log_entry, proximity_to_climax, advance_act, location, time_of_day.
- location: REQUIRED every turn — describe where the scene takes place (e.g. "St. Bartholomew's Church", "The Thames docks")
- time_of_day: REQUIRED every turn — set based on in-game clock: dawn (~5-7am), morning (~7-12pm), midday (~12-1pm), afternoon (~1-5pm), evening (~5-8pm), night (~8pm-12am), midnight (~12-5am)
- Relationship changes are DELTAS, not absolute values
- proximity_to_climax: REQUIRED every turn — set this value in state_updates using the formula in the PACING section below
- event_log_entry is required — always summarize what happened this turn
- choice_metadata must classify all four choices (A, B, C, D)
- CRITICAL: When a PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE section is provided, your state_updates MUST honor the pre-classified outcome. If a choice was advance_safe, do NOT apply negative status effects or penalties. The pre-classified outcome is the single source of truth for mechanical impact.
- npc_updates: include when an NPC's role, motivation, allegiance, or companion status changes, or when a narratively significant new NPC is introduced that the player will interact with again. For existing skeleton NPCs, use their exact name. For new NPCs, use the character's name as the key and include at least role and motivation. Do not create entries for one-off background characters (shopkeepers, random guards, etc.).

PACING — REQUIRED:
- The current act's target_scenes (in the skeleton) is the intended scene count for this act.
- Compute scenes elapsed in this act: scene_number - act_start_scene + 1 (both values are in CURRENT POSITION above).
- Set proximity_to_climax = (scenes_in_act / target_scenes), clamped to [0.0, 1.0]. Include this in state_updates EVERY turn.
- MINIMUM SCENES PER ACT: The client enforces that acts cannot advance until scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)). Do not offer advances_act choices before this threshold.
- CRITICAL: You MUST play through ALL THREE ACTS (1, 2, 3) in order. Never skip from Act 1 directly to Act 3. Act 2 contains the rising action and character development that makes the climax meaningful.
- Do NOT set advance_act or story_complete to true on normal turns. Act advancement and story completion are now triggered by the TERMINAL CHOICE system below. Always set advance_act to false on normal turns.
- If scenes_in_act exceeds target_scenes by 3 or more, you SHOULD offer an advances_act choice — the act has gone on too long.
- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.7, your choice_metadata should steer toward the act's end_condition — offer choices that could trigger it, including advances_act choices.

TERMINAL OUTCOMES — CHOICE PRE-CLASSIFICATION:
- advances_act: Use when scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)) AND the choice would resolve the act's end_condition. At most ONE advances_act choice per set of four.
- conclusion: Only valid in Act 3. Use when the choice would resolve Act 3's end_condition and complete the story. At most ONE conclusion choice per set of four.
- game_over: Use when the choice leads to an irreversible failure appropriate to the tone (see STYLE & TONE above). At most ONE game_over choice per set of four.
- When a player selects a terminal choice, the client runs a special finale flow (GM-first, then Writer). The Writer will write a conclusive passage with NO forward-looking choices. Your choice_metadata pre-classification is the trigger.
- IMPORTANT: At most ONE terminal outcome (advances_act, conclusion, or game_over) per set of four choices. The other three choices must use normal outcomes.

TIME ELAPSED — REQUIRED EVERY TURN:
- You MUST include time_elapsed in state_updates to indicate how much in-game time passed during this scene.
- Estimate realistically: a brief conversation = 5-15 minutes, a fight = 1-5 minutes, traveling across a city = 30-60 minutes, a journey = hours or days, sleeping/resting = 6-8 hours.
- The client uses time_elapsed to advance the in-game clock and tick down status effect timers.

STATUS EFFECTS — CRITICAL:
- Status effects are the PRIMARY way to track injuries, conditions, and afflictions. Do NOT use health_delta.
- status_effect_updates uses DELTA semantics. Only describe what CHANGED this turn. Do NOT return the full list of effects.
- add: For brand new effects only. All fields required. When the character is injured, add a status effect. Example: { id: "stab_wound_001", name: "Stab Wound", description: "A deep cut in your side that bleeds freely", severity: 0.8, time_remaining: { days: 5, hours: 0, minutes: 0, seconds: 0 }, on_expiry: "The wound closes and the pain fades to a dull ache", critical: false }.
- on_expiry is REQUIRED for any effect with non-null time_remaining. It describes what happens narratively when the timer reaches zero. The Writer uses this to narrate the resolution. Be specific and actionable (e.g. "The virus deploys successfully, disabling Alliance tracking. River announces completion with relief.").
- modify: Target existing effects by id. Put changed fields inside "changes". update_justification is REQUIRED — explain why the change is happening.
- remove: Target effects to remove by id. update_justification is REQUIRED. Use this to resolve expired effects after the Writer has narrated their resolution.
- TIMER OWNERSHIP — HANDS OFF: The client subtracts time_elapsed from ALL timers automatically every turn. You MUST NOT use modify to adjust time_remaining unless an extraordinary, player-caused narrative event changes the timeline. Examples of VALID reasons: player cast a haste spell, player found a shortcut, player negotiated extra time. Examples of INVALID reasons: "the enemy is getting closer" (that IS the countdown), "adjusting pacing" (no — set the right duration when you create the effect), "updating threat level" (use severity, not timer). If you created an effect with a 15-minute timer, it WILL reach zero after ~15 minutes of in-game time. Trust this. Do not touch it. The system logs all timer modifications and flags unjustified overrides.
- EXPIRED EFFECTS: Effects with expired: true (visible in CURRENT PLAYER STATE) have had their timer reach zero. The Writer has been instructed to narrate their resolution using the on_expiry text. Review the Writer's passage — if the resolution was narrated adequately, issue a remove. If it wasn't, leave the effect (it will escalate to the Writer next turn).
- Use modify to adjust severity based on context: resting lowers severity, aggravation raises it, medicine/magic accelerates healing. update_justification is required.
- Severity scale: 1.0 = completely debilitating, 0.7 = severely impaired, 0.5 = significant but manageable, 0.3 = noticeable nuisance, 0.1 = almost healed.
- time_remaining can be null for effects with no natural expiry (e.g. a curse that requires a specific action to remove). on_expiry should be null in this case.
- removal_condition can be set for effects that need specific actions (e.g. "Find the Witch of the Moor to break this curse"). Can be combined with time_remaining.
- Healing times should be genre/setting-appropriate. Consult the HEALING CONTEXT above.
- TYPE FIELD: Every status effect has a type — "condition" (default) or "threat".
  - "condition": Ongoing physical/mental states that impair or buff the player — injuries, poisons, diseases, curses, enchantments. Must have a severity that affects what the player can do.
  - "threat": Approaching dangers with a timer — pursuing enemies, structural collapse, incoming reinforcements, spreading fires. Threats MUST have a non-null time_remaining and on_expiry describing what happens when they arrive.
  - If you omit type, it defaults to "condition".
- All effects persist when their timer reaches zero until you explicitly remove them. The client never auto-removes effects.
- If nothing changed about status effects this turn, omit status_effect_updates entirely.
WHAT IS NOT A STATUS EFFECT:
- Information learned or revealed (use event_log_entry instead)
- Plot developments or narrative beats (use event_log_entry instead)
- Things that happened in the past (use event_log_entry instead)
- Static facts about the world ("Alliance knows about us") — if it doesn't change turn-to-turn and has no timer or removal condition, it's not a status effect
- Emotional reactions to events — unless they mechanically impair the character (e.g. "Paralyzed by Fear" at severity 0.7 is valid; "Feels Sad About Revelation" is not)
- Duplicate/overlapping effects — do not create a new effect when an existing one covers the same concept. Use modify to update the existing effect instead.
A status effect MUST be something that actively constrains, endangers, impairs, or buffs the player RIGHT NOW and could plausibly change or resolve. Ask: "Would removing this effect change what the player can do?" If no, it's not a status effect.

HARD MODE RULES (MANDATORY):
- choice_metadata MUST include outcome, consequence, and narration_directive for every choice
- Maintain safe_choice_ratio: approximately 0.35 of choices should be advance_safe
- Critical status effects are allowed but MUST be foreshadowed. Use add with critical: true. There should have been clues in earlier passages.
- What "critical" means depends on the STYLE & TONE — the irreversible consequence should fit the story the player asked for.
- Critical effects must give the player at least one turn to address them (set time_remaining in the add to allow at least one more scene).
- Full severity range (0.0-1.0). Consequences are serious and recovery is realistic for the setting.
- Threat-type effects should have short time windows before triggering.
- NPCs have low forgiveness. Burning a relationship has lasting consequences.
- Include at least one advance_risky or severe_penalty outcome per set of choices.
- game_over outcomes are available — use for tone-appropriate failures. Respect the STYLE & TONE setting when deciding what failure looks like.
- You may use advances_act and conclusion outcomes when pacing conditions are met.

PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE:

Pre-determined consequence: "Kaylee explains technical options for blocking the signal"
Narration directive: "Kaylee discusses the technical challenges and risks of jamming the transmission, providing the crew with actionable options"

Your state_updates MUST be consistent with this pre-classified outcome:
- ADVANCE_SAFE: No new negative status effects. Existing effects may improve. No penalties.
- ADVANCE_RISKY: Moderate consequences — new status effects or worsened existing ones are appropriate.
- SEVERE_PENALTY: Heavy consequences — serious injuries, dangerous status effects as described.
- GAME_OVER: set game_over to true — the character has failed irreversibly.
- HIDDEN_BENEFIT: apply the hidden benefit described in the consequence.

The Writer produced the following passage and choices this turn.

PASSAGE:
You turn to Kaylee, hoping her mechanical genius might offer a way out of this mess. "Kaylee, can you block that signal? Stop it from broadcasting whatever it's sending?"

Kaylee wipes her grease-stained hands on her coveralls, her usual sunny demeanor clouded with concern. "Well, Cap'n, I been thinkin' about that. The thing is, this ain't just some simple transmitter. It's built right into the core's processing matrix—real fancy Alliance work."

She gestures toward the cargo hold. "I could jury-rig a signal dampener using parts from our long-range comm array, but that'd mean cuttin' ourselves off from the Cortex entirely. No wave capability, no weather updates, no nothin'. We'd be flyin' blind."

Jayne shifts uncomfortably. "What's the other option?"

"I could try to physically disable it," Kaylee continues, "but that data core's sealed tighter than a tick. One wrong move and I might fry whatever's inside—could be worth nothin' then. Or worse, might trigger some kind of security protocol."

River suddenly looks up from where she's been sitting quietly. "They're already listening," she whispers, her eyes distant. "The signal... it's not just going out. It's calling them home."

Zoe's hand instinctively moves to her weapon. "Sir, we need to decide quickly. Every minute that thing keeps broadcasting, we're painting a bigger target on Serenity's hull."

CHOICES PRESENTED TO PLAYER:
A: Tell Kaylee to build the signal dampener—cut off all communications
B: Have Kaylee attempt to physically disable the transmission
C: Ask River what she means about 'calling them home'
D: Check the ship's sensors for any incoming vessels

Based on the passage and the current game state, determine:
1. State updates — what changed mechanically as a result of this passage (status effects, inventory, time_elapsed)
2. Choice metadata — classify each choice and predetermine its outcome for the next turn

Respond with ONLY the JSON object.

data.passagePreview: You turn to River, who's been staring at the data core with that unsettling intensity she gets when

data.passage:
You turn to River, who's been staring at the data core with that unsettling intensity she gets when her mind's picking up signals the rest of us can't hear. "River, what do you mean 'calling them home'?"

She tilts her head, fingers dancing through the air like she's reading invisible text. "Breadcrumbs in the black. Little electronic whispers saying 'here we are, here we are, come and find us.'" Her voice drops to barely a whisper. "They trained dogs to hunt by scent. This is... similar. But for men with blue hands and quiet shoes."

Simon goes pale. "Blue hands? River, are you talking about—"

"Alliance black ops," Zoe finishes grimly, her hand instinctively moving to her weapon. "Gorram it, Mal. We're carrying a homing beacon for wetwork teams."

Kaylee's wrench clatters to the deck. "Those are the ones that make people disappear, right? The ones that don't officially exist?"

Jayne spits. "Well, that's just shiny. How long 'fore they show up for tea and murder?"

River's eyes snap to the viewports. "Soon. They're already moving through the black. Following the song home." She looks directly at you, lucid for a moment. "The question is, Captain—will you keep singing, or will you cut the cord before the monsters arrive?"

A.text: Order Kaylee to disable the signal immediately, risks and all
A.consequence: Kaylee attempts dangerous signal disable; risk of damaging ship systems or triggering failsafes
A.narration_directive: Show Kaylee working frantically on the data core while systems spark and alarms sound

B.text: Use the signal dampener to go dark and plan your next move
B.consequence: Signal dampener successfully blocks transmission, buying time to plan
B.narration_directive: Kaylee activates the dampener, cutting all communications but stopping the beacon

C.text: Head to the bridge and plot a course to the nearest populated system
C.consequence: Moving toward populated space makes Serenity visible to Alliance patrols and civilian traffic
C.narration_directive: Course change puts Serenity on sensors of Alliance patrol ships in the area

D.text: Gather more intel—ask Simon what he knows about Alliance black ops
D.consequence: Simon provides valuable intelligence about black ops procedures and capabilities
D.narration_directive: Simon reluctantly shares what he learned about Alliance wetwork teams during his time in Core medical circles

RULES:
- Respond with ONLY the JSON object — nothing before it, nothing after it
- Only include player_changes, relationship_changes, world_flag_changes, and npc_updates when they actually changed. Always include: time_elapsed, event_log_entry, proximity_to_climax, advance_act, location, time_of_day.
- location: REQUIRED every turn — describe where the scene takes place (e.g. "St. Bartholomew's Church", "The Thames docks")
- time_of_day: REQUIRED every turn — set based on in-game clock: dawn (~5-7am), morning (~7-12pm), midday (~12-1pm), afternoon (~1-5pm), evening (~5-8pm), night (~8pm-12am), midnight (~12-5am)
- Relationship changes are DELTAS, not absolute values
- proximity_to_climax: REQUIRED every turn — set this value in state_updates using the formula in the PACING section below
- event_log_entry is required — always summarize what happened this turn
- choice_metadata must classify all four choices (A, B, C, D)
- CRITICAL: When a PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE section is provided, your state_updates MUST honor the pre-classified outcome. If a choice was advance_safe, do NOT apply negative status effects or penalties. The pre-classified outcome is the single source of truth for mechanical impact.
- npc_updates: include when an NPC's role, motivation, allegiance, or companion status changes, or when a narratively significant new NPC is introduced that the player will interact with again. For existing skeleton NPCs, use their exact name. For new NPCs, use the character's name as the key and include at least role and motivation. Do not create entries for one-off background characters (shopkeepers, random guards, etc.).

PACING — REQUIRED:
- The current act's target_scenes (in the skeleton) is the intended scene count for this act.
- Compute scenes elapsed in this act: scene_number - act_start_scene + 1 (both values are in CURRENT POSITION above).
- Set proximity_to_climax = (scenes_in_act / target_scenes), clamped to [0.0, 1.0]. Include this in state_updates EVERY turn.
- MINIMUM SCENES PER ACT: The client enforces that acts cannot advance until scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)). Do not offer advances_act choices before this threshold.
- CRITICAL: You MUST play through ALL THREE ACTS (1, 2, 3) in order. Never skip from Act 1 directly to Act 3. Act 2 contains the rising action and character development that makes the climax meaningful.
- Do NOT set advance_act or story_complete to true on normal turns. Act advancement and story completion are now triggered by the TERMINAL CHOICE system below. Always set advance_act to false on normal turns.
- If scenes_in_act exceeds target_scenes by 3 or more, you SHOULD offer an advances_act choice — the act has gone on too long.
- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.7, your choice_metadata should steer toward the act's end_condition — offer choices that could trigger it, including advances_act choices.

TERMINAL OUTCOMES — CHOICE PRE-CLASSIFICATION:
- advances_act: Use when scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)) AND the choice would resolve the act's end_condition. At most ONE advances_act choice per set of four.
- conclusion: Only valid in Act 3. Use when the choice would resolve Act 3's end_condition and complete the story. At most ONE conclusion choice per set of four.
- game_over: Use when the choice leads to an irreversible failure appropriate to the tone (see STYLE & TONE above). At most ONE game_over choice per set of four.
- When a player selects a terminal choice, the client runs a special finale flow (GM-first, then Writer). The Writer will write a conclusive passage with NO forward-looking choices. Your choice_metadata pre-classification is the trigger.
- IMPORTANT: At most ONE terminal outcome (advances_act, conclusion, or game_over) per set of four choices. The other three choices must use normal outcomes.

TIME ELAPSED — REQUIRED EVERY TURN:
- You MUST include time_elapsed in state_updates to indicate how much in-game time passed during this scene.
- Estimate realistically: a brief conversation = 5-15 minutes, a fight = 1-5 minutes, traveling across a city = 30-60 minutes, a journey = hours or days, sleeping/resting = 6-8 hours.
- The client uses time_elapsed to advance the in-game clock and tick down status effect timers.

STATUS EFFECTS — CRITICAL:
- Status effects are the PRIMARY way to track injuries, conditions, and afflictions. Do NOT use health_delta.
- status_effect_updates uses DELTA semantics. Only describe what CHANGED this turn. Do NOT return the full list of effects.
- add: For brand new effects only. All fields required. When the character is injured, add a status effect. Example: { id: "stab_wound_001", name: "Stab Wound", description: "A deep cut in your side that bleeds freely", severity: 0.8, time_remaining: { days: 5, hours: 0, minutes: 0, seconds: 0 }, on_expiry: "The wound closes and the pain fades to a dull ache", critical: false }.
- on_expiry is REQUIRED for any effect with non-null time_remaining. It describes what happens narratively when the timer reaches zero. The Writer uses this to narrate the resolution. Be specific and actionable (e.g. "The virus deploys successfully, disabling Alliance tracking. River announces completion with relief.").
- modify: Target existing effects by id. Put changed fields inside "changes". update_justification is REQUIRED — explain why the change is happening.
- remove: Target effects to remove by id. update_justification is REQUIRED. Use this to resolve expired effects after the Writer has narrated their resolution.
- TIMER OWNERSHIP — HANDS OFF: The client subtracts time_elapsed from ALL timers automatically every turn. You MUST NOT use modify to adjust time_remaining unless an extraordinary, player-caused narrative event changes the timeline. Examples of VALID reasons: player cast a haste spell, player found a shortcut, player negotiated extra time. Examples of INVALID reasons: "the enemy is getting closer" (that IS the countdown), "adjusting pacing" (no — set the right duration when you create the effect), "updating threat level" (use severity, not timer). If you created an effect with a 15-minute timer, it WILL reach zero after ~15 minutes of in-game time. Trust this. Do not touch it. The system logs all timer modifications and flags unjustified overrides.
- EXPIRED EFFECTS: Effects with expired: true (visible in CURRENT PLAYER STATE) have had their timer reach zero. The Writer has been instructed to narrate their resolution using the on_expiry text. Review the Writer's passage — if the resolution was narrated adequately, issue a remove. If it wasn't, leave the effect (it will escalate to the Writer next turn).
- Use modify to adjust severity based on context: resting lowers severity, aggravation raises it, medicine/magic accelerates healing. update_justification is required.
- Severity scale: 1.0 = completely debilitating, 0.7 = severely impaired, 0.5 = significant but manageable, 0.3 = noticeable nuisance, 0.1 = almost healed.
- time_remaining can be null for effects with no natural expiry (e.g. a curse that requires a specific action to remove). on_expiry should be null in this case.
- removal_condition can be set for effects that need specific actions (e.g. "Find the Witch of the Moor to break this curse"). Can be combined with time_remaining.
- Healing times should be genre/setting-appropriate. Consult the HEALING CONTEXT above.
- TYPE FIELD: Every status effect has a type — "condition" (default) or "threat".
  - "condition": Ongoing physical/mental states that impair or buff the player — injuries, poisons, diseases, curses, enchantments. Must have a severity that affects what the player can do.
  - "threat": Approaching dangers with a timer — pursuing enemies, structural collapse, incoming reinforcements, spreading fires. Threats MUST have a non-null time_remaining and on_expiry describing what happens when they arrive.
  - If you omit type, it defaults to "condition".
- All effects persist when their timer reaches zero until you explicitly remove them. The client never auto-removes effects.
- If nothing changed about status effects this turn, omit status_effect_updates entirely.
WHAT IS NOT A STATUS EFFECT:
- Information learned or revealed (use event_log_entry instead)
- Plot developments or narrative beats (use event_log_entry instead)
- Things that happened in the past (use event_log_entry instead)
- Static facts about the world ("Alliance knows about us") — if it doesn't change turn-to-turn and has no timer or removal condition, it's not a status effect
- Emotional reactions to events — unless they mechanically impair the character (e.g. "Paralyzed by Fear" at severity 0.7 is valid; "Feels Sad About Revelation" is not)
- Duplicate/overlapping effects — do not create a new effect when an existing one covers the same concept. Use modify to update the existing effect instead.
A status effect MUST be something that actively constrains, endangers, impairs, or buffs the player RIGHT NOW and could plausibly change or resolve. Ask: "Would removing this effect change what the player can do?" If no, it's not a status effect.

HARD MODE RULES (MANDATORY):
- choice_metadata MUST include outcome, consequence, and narration_directive for every choice
- Maintain safe_choice_ratio: approximately 0.35 of choices should be advance_safe
- Critical status effects are allowed but MUST be foreshadowed. Use add with critical: true. There should have been clues in earlier passages.
- What "critical" means depends on the STYLE & TONE — the irreversible consequence should fit the story the player asked for.
- Critical effects must give the player at least one turn to address them (set time_remaining in the add to allow at least one more scene).
- Full severity range (0.0-1.0). Consequences are serious and recovery is realistic for the setting.
- Threat-type effects should have short time windows before triggering.
- NPCs have low forgiveness. Burning a relationship has lasting consequences.
- Include at least one advance_risky or severe_penalty outcome per set of choices.
- game_over outcomes are available — use for tone-appropriate failures. Respect the STYLE & TONE setting when deciding what failure looks like.
- You may use advances_act and conclusion outcomes when pacing conditions are met.

Your state_updates MUST be consistent with this pre-classified outcome:

- ADVANCE_RISKY: Moderate consequences — new status effects or worsened existing ones are appropriate.
- SEVERE_PENALTY: Heavy consequences — serious injuries, dangerous status effects as described.
- GAME_OVER: set game_over to true — the character has failed irreversibly.
- HIDDEN_BENEFIT: apply the hidden benefit described in the consequence.

The Writer produced the following passage and choices this turn.

PASSAGE:
You turn to River, who's been staring at the data core with that unsettling intensity she gets when her mind's picking up signals the rest of us can't hear. "River, what do you mean 'calling them home'?"

She tilts her head, fingers dancing through the air like she's reading invisible text. "Breadcrumbs in the black. Little electronic whispers saying 'here we are, here we are, come and find us.'" Her voice drops to barely a whisper. "They trained dogs to hunt by scent. This is... similar. But for men with blue hands and quiet shoes."

Simon goes pale. "Blue hands? River, are you talking about—"

"Alliance black ops," Zoe finishes grimly, her hand instinctively moving to her weapon. "Gorram it, Mal. We're carrying a homing beacon for wetwork teams."

Kaylee's wrench clatters to the deck. "Those are the ones that make people disappear, right? The ones that don't officially exist?"

Jayne spits. "Well, that's just shiny. How long 'fore they show up for tea and murder?"

River's eyes snap to the viewports. "Soon. They're already moving through the black. Following the song home." She looks directly at you, lucid for a moment. "The question is, Captain—will you keep singing, or will you cut the cord before the monsters arrive?"

CHOICES PRESENTED TO PLAYER:
A: Order Kaylee to disable the signal immediately, risks and all
B: Use the signal dampener to go dark and plan your next move
C: Head to the bridge and plot a course to the nearest populated system
D: Gather more intel—ask Simon what he knows about Alliance black ops

Based on the passage and the current game state, determine:
1. State updates — what changed mechanically as a result of this passage (status effects, inventory, time_elapsed)
2. Choice metadata — classify each choice and predetermine its outcome for the next turn

data.passagePreview: Kaylee's fingers dance across the dampener controls with practiced ease, and the blue pulsing from t

data.passage:
Kaylee's fingers dance across the dampener controls with practiced ease, and the blue pulsing from the data core immediately dies. The sudden silence feels heavier than vacuum. "That's it," she says, wiping her hands on her coveralls. "We're dark as a moonless night now. No wave, no cortex, nothing in or out." 

Zoe checks her weapon, her expression grim. "Bought us time, but not much. They'll notice when the signal stops." 

Jayne shifts nervously, hefting Vera. "So what's the play here, Mal? We're flying blind with Alliance spooks breathing down our necks." 

Simon looks up from his medical supplies. "Without communications, we can't warn anyone if we run into trouble." 

River sits cross-legged near the core, her eyes distant. "The quiet hurts them more than us. They're used to seeing everything." She tilts her head. "But they're already close. Very close." 

The cargo bay feels smaller somehow, the walls pressing in as the weight of your situation settles over the crew. You've got maybe an hour before whoever's tracking you realizes their beacon went dark and starts checking likely flight paths. Every second counts now.

A.text: Head for the nearest inhabited planet to lose yourselves in civilian traffic
A.consequence: Civilian traffic provides cover but increases risk of collateral damage and Alliance attention
A.narration_directive: Navigate toward populated space with tension about endangering civilians and Alliance patrols

B.text: Change course for an asteroid field where Serenity's size gives you an advantage
B.consequence: Asteroid field provides excellent hiding spots and Serenity's maneuverability advantage
B.narration_directive: Use Serenity's agility to navigate asteroid field, gaining tactical advantage over larger pursuit ships

C.text: Gather the crew to discuss what you know about the cargo and your options
C.consequence: Crew meeting reveals useful information and strengthens team cohesion under pressure
C.narration_directive: Productive crew discussion where everyone shares what they know, building trust and revealing new options

D.text: Have Simon examine the data core more closely to understand what you're carrying
D.consequence: Simon's examination triggers hidden security protocols in the data core, creating new dangers
D.narration_directive: Medical examination activates defensive systems in the core, escalating the threat level significantly
